title: Getting started
text:
Your first step is to clone the Filetransfer Tool Server repository and its submodules
with these commands:
You have two options for building the Filetransfer Tool Server:
Use Docker: Build a Docker image that contains the server.
Use a Manual build (Linux or Windows): Build the server directly on your machine.
To build the Docker image, run the following command from the root of the repository:
To build on Linux, you must have these tools installed:
python3
cmake
g++ compiler
First, you must install the development dependencies:
Then, use Conan to fetch the C++ dependencies:
Finally, use CMake to configure and build the project:
To build on Windows, you must have these tools installed:
python
cmake
MSVC compiler
First, you must install the development dependencies:
Then, use Conan to fetch the C++ dependencies:
Finally, use CMake to configure and build the project:
After the server is built, you can run it in the following ways:
This starts the server and exposes it on port 50000.
To make the uploaded files available to another process, you can share a volume between two Docker containers. A Docker Compose file might look like this:
The Filetransfer Tool Server provides these command-line options:
--help - Display a help message and exit.
--server-address - Configure the address that the server is listening on.
git clone https://github.com/ansys/ansys-tools-filetransfer-server
cd ansys-tools-filetransfer-server
git submodule update --init --recursive
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile . --tag filetransfer-tool-server
python3 -m pip install pipx
pipx ensurepath
pipx install 'poetry>=1.8.0'
poetry install
poetry run conan install -of build --build missing --profile:host=./conan/linux_x86_64_Release --profile:build=./conan/linux_x86_64_Release ./conan
cmake -B build . -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE='build/conan_toolchain.cmake' -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build build --config Release --parallel
python -m pip install pipx
pipx ensurepath
pipx install poetry
poetry install
poetry run conan install -of build --build missing --profile:host=.\conan\windows_x86_64_Release --profile:build=.\conan\windows_x86_64_Release .\conan
cmake -B build . -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE='build/conan_toolchain.cmake' -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build build --config Release --parallel
docker run -p 50000:50000 filetransfer-tool-server
version: '3.8'
services:
  other-service:
    restart: unless-stopped
    image: <other_service_image>
    <any other options needed for this service>
    working_dir: /home/container/workdir
    volumes:
      - "shared_data:/home/container/workdir/"
    user: "1000:1000"
  ansys-tools-filetransfer:
    restart: unless-stopped
    image: filetransfer-tool-server
    ports:
      - "50000:50000"
    working_dir: /home/container/workdir
    volumes:
      - "shared_data:/home/container/workdir/"
    user: "1000:1000"

volumes:
  shared_data:
./build/src/server --server-address localhost:50000
.\build\src\Release\server.exe --server-address localhost:50000

title: Getting started > Run the server
text:
After the server is built, you can run it in the following ways:
This starts the server and exposes it on port 50000.
To make the uploaded files available to another process, you can share a volume between two Docker containers. A Docker Compose file might look like this:
docker run -p 50000:50000 filetransfer-tool-server
version: '3.8'
services:
  other-service:
    restart: unless-stopped
    image: <other_service_image>
    <any other options needed for this service>
    working_dir: /home/container/workdir
    volumes:
      - "shared_data:/home/container/workdir/"
    user: "1000:1000"
  ansys-tools-filetransfer:
    restart: unless-stopped
    image: filetransfer-tool-server
    ports:
      - "50000:50000"
    working_dir: /home/container/workdir
    volumes:
      - "shared_data:/home/container/workdir/"
    user: "1000:1000"

volumes:
  shared_data:
./build/src/server --server-address localhost:50000
.\build\src\Release\server.exe --server-address localhost:50000

title: Getting started > Command-line options
text:
The Filetransfer Tool Server provides these command-line options:
--help - Display a help message and exit.
--server-address - Configure the address that the server is listening on.

title: Contribute
text:
Overall guidance on contributing to a PyAnsys library appears in the
Contributing topic
in the PyAnsys developer’s guide. Ensure that you are thoroughly familiar with
this guide before attempting to contribute to the FileTransfer Tool Server.
For information on how to build the FileTransfer Tool Server from source, see
getting_started.
To build the documentation, use this command:
To run tests, use this command:
To run pre-commit style checks, run this command:
Documentation follows for the internal classes and functions of the FileTransfer Tool Server.
This documentation is intended for developers who want to understand the inner workings
of the server and possibly extend it.
Named constants for the progress of a file transfer operation. 
Values:
This class implements the file transfer service. 
Implements the “DownloadFile” operation. 
context – Server context to use. 
stream – Stream of requests and responses to process. 
Result of the operation. 
Implements the “UploadFile” operation. 
context – Server context to use. 
stream – Stream of requests and responses to process. 
Result of the operation. 
Get the SHA1 hex digest of a file. 
This function is not thread-safe. 
path_ – Path to the file. 
chunk_size_ – Size of the chunks to read from the file. 
SHA1 hex digest of the file.
Convert exceptions to gRPC status codes. 
fun – Function to execute. 
gRPC status code. 
Exception type raised when an object is not found. 
Exception type raised when invalid parameters are provided. 
Exception type raised when some precondition is not met. For example, a file is expected to exist, but it does not. 
Exception type raised when the transmitted data is lost or corrupted. 
Exception type raised when an internal error occurs. 
poetry run -C doc html
cd build; ctest; cd ..
poetry run pre-commit run --all-files

title: Ansys FileTransfer Tool Server documentation
text:
The Ansys FileTransfer Tool is a simple gRPC API for moving files between a
client and a remote server. Its target use case are local Docker deployments or
simple remote deployments.
This documentation describes the server component of the FileTransfer Tool. For
information on the tool itself, see the
FileTransfer Tool documentation.
The FileTransfer Tool does not provide any security measures. Any file
on the server component can be accessed by any client. Without additional security
measures, it is unsuited for use over an untrusted network.
Explains how to build and run the FileTransfer Tool Server.
Explains how to set up a development environment and provides documentation for
the internal classes and functions of the FileTransfer Tool Server for developers
who want to understanding the inner workings of the server and possibly extend it.
